**Mary Jeannie May Simon**[[a]](#cite_note-5) (born August 21, 1947) is a Canadian civil servant, diplomat, and former broadcaster who has been serving as the 30th [governor general of Canada](/wiki/Governor_general_of_Canada "Governor general of Canada") since July 26, 2021. She is [Inuk](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") on her mother's side, making her the first [Indigenous person](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") to hold the office.[[b]](#cite_note-6)

Simon was born in Fort Severight (now [Kangiqsualujjuaq](/wiki/Kangiqsualujjuaq "Kangiqsualujjuaq")), Quebec. She briefly worked as a producer and announcer for the [CBC Northern Service](/wiki/CBC_Northern_Service "CBC Northern Service") in the 1970s before entering public service, serving on the board of the [Northern Quebec Inuit Association](/wiki/Northern_Quebec_Inuit_Association "Northern Quebec Inuit Association") and playing a key role in the [Charlottetown Accord](/wiki/Charlottetown_Accord "Charlottetown Accord") negotiations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] She was Canada's first ambassador for circumpolar affairs from 1994 to 2004, as well as a lead negotiator for the creation of the [Arctic Council](/wiki/Arctic_Council "Arctic Council").[[5]](#cite_note-Biography-7) She also served as the Canadian ambassador to [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") from 1999 to 2002.

On July 6, 2021, Prime Minister [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") announced that [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") had approved Simon's appointment as governor general.[[6]](#cite_note-Prime-8) Her appointment was criticized, as she is not fluent in French, an official language of Canada.[[7]](#cite_note-fr-9)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Simon was born Mary Jeannie May[[4]](#cite_note-GG_Acceptance_Speech-4) on August 21, 1947,[[8]](#cite_note-Ordre-10) in Fort Severight (now [Kangiqsualujjuaq](/wiki/Kangiqsualujjuaq "Kangiqsualujjuaq")), Quebec,[[9]](#cite_note-GGBio-11) to Bob May, who was from [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") and of [English](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") descent,[[10]](#cite_note-12) and to her mother, Nancy, an [Inuk](/wiki/Inuk "Inuk").[[11]](#cite_note-Curry–Raman-Wilms_2021-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Fennell_1995-14) Her father had relocated to the north in his youth[[13]](#cite_note-Diebel_1984-15) and became manager of the local [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") (HBC) store during the early 1950s.[[12]](#cite_note-Fennell_1995-14)[[14]](#cite_note-Hay_1989-16) He said that he was the first white employee to marry an Inuk, which the HBC banned at the time.[[15]](#cite_note-Curry_2007-17) Mary Simon's Inuk name is *Ningiukudluk*, which means "bossy little old lady".[[4]](#cite_note-GG_Acceptance_Speech-4)[[16]](#cite_note-18)

Simon was raised in a traditional [Inuit lifestyle](/wiki/Inuit_culture "Inuit culture"), including hunting, fishing, sewing [Inuit clothing](/wiki/Inuit_clothing "Inuit clothing"), and travelling by [dog sled](/wiki/Dog_sled "Dog sled").[[4]](#cite_note-GG_Acceptance_Speech-4)[[17]](#cite_note-Fisher_1986-19) She credits her mother and maternal grandmother Jeannie Angnatuk for passing on Inuit [oral history](/wiki/Oral_history "Oral history") to her.[[4]](#cite_note-GG_Acceptance_Speech-4)[[11]](#cite_note-Curry–Raman-Wilms_2021-13)

Simon attended federal day school in Fort Chimo (now [Kuujjuaq](/wiki/Kuujjuaq "Kuujjuaq")),[[18]](#cite_note-:0-20) then [Fort Carson High School](/wiki/Fountain-Fort_Carson_High_School "Fountain-Fort Carson High School") in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), and completed her high school via correspondence in Fort Chimo.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

Simon taught [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut "Inuktitut") at [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University").[[19]](#cite_note-Farnsworth_1995-21) From 1969 to 1973, she worked as a producer and announcer for the [CBC Northern Service](/wiki/CBC_Northern_Service "CBC Northern Service").[[20]](#cite_note-22)

Simon began her career as a public servant by being elected secretary of the board of directors of the Northern Quebec Inuit Association. In 1978, she was elected as vice-president, and later president, of the [Makivik Corporation](/wiki/Makivik_Corporation "Makivik Corporation"). She held the position until 1985.[[21]](#cite_note-cana-23)

During this period she also became involved with [Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami](/wiki/Inuit_Tapiriit_Kanatami "Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami"), Canada's national [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") organization.[[22]](#cite_note-onefuture-24) Simon was one of the senior Inuit negotiators during the patriation of the [Canadian Constitution](/wiki/Canadian_Constitution "Canadian Constitution"), the First Ministers' conferences that took place from 1982 to 1992, as well as the 1992 [Charlottetown Accord](/wiki/Charlottetown_Accord "Charlottetown Accord") discussions.

She served as a member of the [Nunavut Implementation Commission](/wiki/Nunavut_Implementation_Commission "Nunavut Implementation Commission") and as co-director (policy) and secretary to the [Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_Aboriginal_Peoples "Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples").[[22]](#cite_note-onefuture-24)

### Diplomatic career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Diplomatic career")]

She took on a variety of roles for the [Inuit Circumpolar Conference](/wiki/Inuit_Circumpolar_Conference "Inuit Circumpolar Conference") (ICC). First she served as an Executive Council member from 1980 to 1983, then as president from 1986 to 1992, and then as Special Envoy from 1992 to 1994.[[22]](#cite_note-onefuture-24) In 1986, as president of the ICC, Simon led a delegation of Canadian, [Alaskan](/wiki/Alaskan "Alaskan"), and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") Inuit to [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") and then to Chukotka to meet with Russian officials as well as the Inuit of the [far east of Russia](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East"). In 1987 the ICC was successful in efforts that resulted in the Russian government allowing [Russian Inuit](/wiki/Siberian_Yupik "Siberian Yupik") to attend the 1989 ICC General Assembly held in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Ambassadorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ambassadorship")]

In 1994, Simon was appointed by Prime Minister [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien") to be Canadian Ambassador for Circumpolar Affairs,[[23]](#cite_note-25) a newly created position she held until early 2004.[[24]](#cite_note-Post-26) Acting on instructions from the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada"), she took the lead role in negotiating the creation of an eight-country council known today as the [Arctic Council](/wiki/Arctic_Council "Arctic Council"). The 1996 [Ottawa Declaration](/wiki/Arctic_Council#History "Arctic Council") formally established it to include the active participation of indigenous peoples in the circumpolar world. Under her leadership, and later as Canada's Senior Arctic Official, she and its [indigenous permanent participants](/wiki/Arctic_Council#Indigenous_permanent_participants "Arctic Council") worked closely with those of its seven other states.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During this time, Simon was also the Canadian Ambassador to Denmark[[25]](#cite_note-27) (1999–2002),[[26]](#cite_note-DENMARK-28) a member of the Joint Public Advisory Committee of the [Commission on Environmental Cooperation](/wiki/Commission_on_Environmental_Cooperation "Commission on Environmental Cooperation") (1997–2000) and its chairwoman from 1997 to 1998.

Simon was appointed Councillor for the International Council for Conflict Resolution at the [Carter Center](/wiki/Carter_Center "Carter Center") in 2001.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Post-diplomatic work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-diplomatic work")]

From November 2004 to February 2005, she assisted with the facilitation and write-up of reports on the "Sectoral Follow-up Sessions" announced by Prime Minister Paul Martin following the April 19, 2004 *Canada-Aboriginal Peoples Roundtable on Strengthening the Relationship on Health, Life Long learning, Housing, Economic Opportunities, Negotiations, and Accountability for Results*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] From June 2004 to June 2007, Simon was a [board member](/wiki/Board_member "Board member") at the [International Institute for Sustainable Development](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Sustainable_Development "International Institute for Sustainable Development").[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)

From 2004 to 2005, Simon was special advisor to the [Labrador Inuit Association](/wiki/Labrador_Inuit_Association "Labrador Inuit Association") on the Labrador Inuit Land Claims Agreement.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32) She was elected president of [Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami](/wiki/Inuit_Tapiriit_Kanatami "Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami") on July 7, 2006.[[31]](#cite_note-Nunatsiaq_News-33)

In 2010, Simon was reported to be under consideration for Governor General of Canada.[[32]](#cite_note-34) [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)") was ultimately appointed.[[33]](#cite_note-Appt2-35)

## Governor General of Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Governor General of Canada")]

### Nomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Nomination")]

Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announcing Mary Simon as the 30th governor general of Canada, 2021

The federal government began a search for a replacement for Governor General [Julie Payette](/wiki/Julie_Payette "Julie Payette") following her resignation in early 2021. Simon was reported as a leading contender for the post early on, given her Indigenous heritage and then-political consciousness on Indigenous reconciliation. On July 6, 2021, Prime Minister [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") announced that Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") had approved Simon's appointment as the 30th Governor General of Canada.[[34]](#cite_note-36) She received a customary audience with the Queen on July 22, though held virtually (instead of in-person) due to the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/Coronavirus_pandemic "Coronavirus pandemic"). She was vested with special appointments as Chancellor of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada"), the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)"), the [Order of Merit of the Police Forces](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_the_Police_Forces "Order of Merit of the Police Forces") and the [Order of St. John](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_John_(chartered_1888) "Order of Saint John (chartered 1888)") (within Canada). She also received the [Canadian Forces' Decoration](/wiki/Canadian_Forces%27_Decoration "Canadian Forces' Decoration"). She was formally installed at the [Senate of Canada Building](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada_Building "Senate of Canada Building") on July 26.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

Simon's appointment was somewhat unusual, in that although she speaks English and Inuktitut, she is not particularly proficient in French. This raised some complaints from [Francophone Canadians](/wiki/Francophone_Canadians "Francophone Canadians") for breaking the tradition of [French-English bilingualism](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada").[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[7]](#cite_note-fr-9)

### Tenure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Tenure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Platinum_Jubilee-_A_Canadian_bilateral.jpg/250px-Platinum_Jubilee-_A_Canadian_bilateral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Platinum_Jubilee-_A_Canadian_bilateral.jpg)

Simon and Dame [Cindy Kiro](/wiki/Cindy_Kiro "Cindy Kiro"), Governor-General of New Zealand, host a bilateral between Canada and New Zealand during their Platinum Jubilee visit to the United Kingdom, June 2022.

#### *Ajuinnata*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ajuinnata")]

Simon considers the concept of *ajuinnata* as an important theme for her mandate as governor general.[[37]](#cite_note-39) *Ajuinnata* is an Inuktitut word that does not have a one-word translation, as it encompasses many things: a vow or a promise to never give up, or a commitment to action no matter how daunting the cause may be.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Simon said that the word was taught to her by her mother and grandmother, and is an important concept for Inuit.[[39]](#cite_note-41) According to Simon, the spirit of *ajuinnata* drove her to get involved in movements to improve the lives of Inuit in Canada.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

#### Germany visit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Germany visit")]

Simon made her first trip abroad as Governor General on October 17, 2021, when she and her husband arrived in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Germany on a [state visit](/wiki/State_visit "State visit").[[41]](#cite_note-43) The trip was Canada's first state visit to Germany in over 20 years.[[42]](#cite_note-44) During her visit, Simon met with [President of Germany](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany") [Frank-Walter Steinmeier](/wiki/Frank-Walter_Steinmeier "Frank-Walter Steinmeier") and [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel").[[43]](#cite_note-:1-45) In an interview with *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")*, Simon said she discussed with Steinmeier, how in fulfilling the role of head of state, to express and atone for the darkest moments of their countries' history—[the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and the [Residential School System](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_system "Canadian Indian residential school system").[[44]](#cite_note-46) She also attended the [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") and a roundtable discussion on [Arctic exploration](/wiki/Arctic_exploration "Arctic exploration") at the Frankfurt Archaeological Museum.[[43]](#cite_note-:1-45)

#### Queen's Platinum Jubilee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Queen's Platinum Jubilee")]

On [Accession Day](/wiki/Accession_Day "Accession Day"), February 6, 2022, Simon paid tribute to the Queen in a message to mark her Platinum Jubilee. She said:[[45]](#cite_note-47)

> Much has changed in the last seven decades. We extended the hand of friendship to nations around the world. We made advancements in medical research, most recently with vaccines. We established the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and took part in its work. We saw the first Canadian named governor general, then the first woman and now, the first Indigenous person.

Simon and her husband met the Queen for the first time on March 15, 2022, at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"). The Queen hosted afternoon tea for the couple. It was the first time that the Canadian monarch met the first indigenous governor general in Canadian history. Simon later said in an interview that she and the Queen discussed various issues like the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), [Canada convoy protests](/wiki/Canada_convoy_protests "Canada convoy protests"), and how they both recovered from [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"). Simon said she told the Queen that Canada's history books should be rewritten to reflect the facts about the relationship between [the Canadian Crown and Indigenous peoples of Canada](/wiki/The_Canadian_Crown_and_Indigenous_peoples_of_Canada "The Canadian Crown and Indigenous peoples of Canada").[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49)

In May, Simon hosted [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") and [Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla") on their Platinum Jubilee tour of Canada. During the tour, Simon invested Charles as an Extraordinary Commander of the Order of Military Merit at Rideau Hall.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Platinum_Jubilee-_Dame_Cindy_with_Governors-General_from_Canada_and_Australia.jpg/250px-Platinum_Jubilee-_Dame_Cindy_with_Governors-General_from_Canada_and_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Platinum_Jubilee-_Dame_Cindy_with_Governors-General_from_Canada_and_Australia.jpg)

Simon with governors-general [David Hurley](/wiki/David_Hurley "David Hurley") of Australia and [Dame Cindy Kiro](/wiki/Dame_Cindy_Kiro "Dame Cindy Kiro") of New Zealand, outside St Paul's Cathedral, London, June 3, 2022

Simon and her husband travelled to London from June 2 to 6, 2022, to take part in [the Queen's Platinum Jubilee](/wiki/The_Queen%27s_Platinum_Jubilee "The Queen's Platinum Jubilee") celebrations in the United Kingdom. They attended the [Service of Thanksgiving](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_National_Service_of_Thanksgiving "Platinum Jubilee National Service of Thanksgiving") at [St. Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Cathedral "St. Paul's Cathedral"), the [Platinum Party at the Palace](/wiki/Platinum_Party_at_the_Palace "Platinum Party at the Palace"), and the [Platinum Jubilee Pageant](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_Pageant "Platinum Jubilee Pageant"), which included military personnel from the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces").[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Simon urged all Canadians to work together, to "truly honour the life, legacy and reign of Her Majesty The Queen", and said that, to her, that is worth celebrating.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

#### Papal visit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Papal visit")]

On April 1, 2022, Simon released a statement following [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis")' apology to Indigenous delegation at the [Vatican](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). Simon said she was grateful to the Pope for his words, and hailed it a "historic and emotional day for Indigenous peoples across Canada". She said that the apology is "one step on the road to reconciliation", and the Pope has "committed to visiting Canada to continue the reconciliation journey with Indigenous peoples on their own lands".[[51]](#cite_note-53)

Pope Francis visited Canada from July 24 to 29, 2022, with Simon taking part in several events and activities during the papal visit.[[52]](#cite_note-54) Following the papal apology in [Maskwacis](/wiki/Maskwacis "Maskwacis") on July 25, Simon said, "Today was a day that moved us forward, giving Survivors words that may help them heal. Yet it is also a day that can raise complex emotions, especially as the Papal visit continues".[[53]](#cite_note-55)

#### Transition of the Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transition of the Crown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Realms_Lunch_Coronation_Event_%2852872267136%29.jpg/250px-Realms_Lunch_Coronation_Event_%2852872267136%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Realms_Lunch_Coronation_Event_(52872267136).jpg)

Simon meeting King Charles III at Buckingham Palace, May 5, 2023

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Governor_General_en_route_at_Rideau_Hall%2C_2025.jpg/250px-Governor_General_en_route_at_Rideau_Hall%2C_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Governor_General_en_route_at_Rideau_Hall,_2025.jpg)

Simon during the [2025 royal tour of Canada](/wiki/2025_royal_tour_of_Canada "2025 royal tour of Canada")

With Queen Elizabeth II's death on September 8, 2022, Simon became the first governor general to serve under two monarchs since [Lord Tweedsmuir](/wiki/John_Buchan "John Buchan") in 1936. Simon said, "Her Majesty's warm welcome when we spent time with her earlier this year was a profound moment in our lives and a memory we will cherish forever".[[54]](#cite_note-56) In a live statement to Canadians, Simon said, "Her Majesty cared about people, about our well-being. This was clear every time we spoke. She cared about Canada, and all the unique stories that make up our beautiful country".[[55]](#cite_note-57)

On September 10, Simon signed the proclamation of the accession of King Charles III at Rideau Hall following a formal meeting of the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada").[[56]](#cite_note-58) In a statement, Simon said, "the Crown endures and thrives as a symbol of service, tradition and commitment. His Majesty The King ascends at an important time in history for Canada and the Commonwealth".[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Simon and her husband were part of the Canadian delegation to the Queen's state funeral in London on September 19.[[58]](#cite_note-60) On the occasion of the Queen's funeral, Simon said, "We were fortunate to have The Queen with us for so long. On behalf of all Canadians, I would like to thank our Queen, our monarch, one last time, for her love and her commitment to our country and our people".[[59]](#cite_note-61)

In May 2023, just days before the [coronation of King Charles III](/wiki/Coronation_of_King_Charles_III "Coronation of King Charles III"), Simon organised an audience at Buckingham Palace between the King and three Indigenous leaders: Assembly of First Nations National Chief [RoseAnne Archibald](/wiki/RoseAnne_Archibald "RoseAnne Archibald"), President of Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami [Natan Obed](/wiki/Natan_Obed "Natan Obed"), and President of the Métis National Council [Cassidy Caron](/wiki/Cassidy_Caron "Cassidy Caron").[[60]](#cite_note-62) Simon said that the King "understands the importance of walking the path of reconciliation with Canada and Indigenous peoples".[[61]](#cite_note-63)

Simon and her husband attended a reception hosted by the King on May 5, and the Coronation Service at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") on May 6. For the coronation, Simon wore a Canadian creation by a Nunavik born artist.[[62]](#cite_note-64) In a statement, Simon said:[[63]](#cite_note-65)

> Seventy years have passed since the last Coronation, offering us an opportunity to reflect on all that has changed in our country, the Commonwealth and the world around us. And so much has changed. The evolution of technology, society and economics is happening at a quickening pace. And while the Crown has also evolved during this time, it continues to be an anchor for our robust and stable democracy and our diverse country.

On May 7, 2023, Simon attended the [Coronation Concert](/wiki/Coronation_Concert "Coronation Concert") at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle").[[64]](#cite_note-66)

#### Expenses criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Expenses criticism")]

In June 2022, Simon came under fire after a *[National Post](/wiki/National_Post "National Post")* story was published regarding exorbitant in-flight catering costs of over $93,000 for her eight-day trip to the Middle East.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) She called the criticism "unfair" as she had no part in the logistics of her trips to discuss world peace but her office would try to "minimize the cost of future voyages".[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) Due to a growing number of abusive comments, in February 2023 the office of the Governor General closed commenting on social media posts.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Simon is the second-oldest of eight children.[[12]](#cite_note-Fennell_1995-14) Her brother, [Johnny May](/wiki/Johnny_May "Johnny May"), is a locally renowned [bush pilot](/wiki/Bush_pilot "Bush pilot").[[18]](#cite_note-:0-20) In her youth, Simon attended an [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") church with her family.[[13]](#cite_note-Diebel_1984-15) At age 40, she developed [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)") and [burnout](/wiki/Occupational_burnout "Occupational burnout"), but gradually overcame them.[[15]](#cite_note-Curry_2007-17)

Simon married her first husband, Robert Otis, on March 27, 1967, in Kuujjuaq.[[70]](#cite_note-72) She later married George Simon,[[13]](#cite_note-Diebel_1984-15) and in 1994 she married her current husband, journalist and author [Whit Fraser](/wiki/Whit_Fraser "Whit Fraser"), a former head of the [Canadian Polar Commission](/wiki/Polar_Knowledge_Canada "Polar Knowledge Canada").[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-74) She has two sons and one daughter.[[13]](#cite_note-Diebel_1984-15) Simon speaks English and Inuktitut. She committed to learn French during her tenure as governor general, though, as of 2024, there were still concerns about her inability to speak the language.[[73]](#cite_note-CBC20210706-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76)

## Current positions and memberships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Current positions and memberships")]

Simon is a fellow of the [Arctic Institute of North America](/wiki/Arctic_Institute_of_North_America "Arctic Institute of North America") and of the [Royal Canadian Geographical Society](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Geographical_Society "Royal Canadian Geographical Society").[[110]](#cite_note-Trent_Bio-112)

She holds other positions, including the following:

* Advisor to the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (Arctic Monitoring Program)
* Chairperson, Arctic Children and Youth Foundation
* Board Member, [Indspire](/wiki/Indspire "Indspire")
* Member of the Board of Governors, [University of the Arctic](/wiki/University_of_the_Arctic "University of the Arctic")
* Council Member, Crossing Boundaries National Council
* Member of Advisory Circle, Walter and Duncan Gordon Charitable Foundation[[111]](#cite_note-113)
* Council Member, The National Police Services Advisory Council

## Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Simon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Arms")]

Coat of arms of Mary Simon

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Notes  Simon was granted armorial bearings by the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority") following her appointment as governor general. The elements of the achievement were chosen to reflect Simon's upbringing and career. The shape of the [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") resembles an *[amauti](/wiki/Amauti "Amauti")*, a traditional parka worn by Inuit women.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115)  Adopted  August 15, 2022  Crest  A [snowy owl](/wiki/Snowy_owl "Snowy owl") affronty wings displayed and inverted environed by [caribou](/wiki/Caribou "Caribou") antlers proper  Escutcheon  Argent an annulet, overall a fess nowy Azure charged with the Royal Crown Argent  Supporters  Two [Arctic foxes](/wiki/Arctic_fox "Arctic fox") proper each gorged of a collar Azure pendent therefrom a hurt that to the dexter charged with a [mountain sorrel](/wiki/Mountain_sorrel "Mountain sorrel") flower, that to the sinister with a cinquefoil Argent, each fox supporting a *[kakivak](/wiki/Kakivak "Kakivak")* and standing on a rocky mount set with a blueberry patch and [cottongrass](/wiki/Cottongrass "Cottongrass") flowers proper  Motto  * ᐊᔪᐃᓐᓇᑕ * *Ajuinnata* ([Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut_language "Inuktitut language") for 'Persevere')  Orders  * Circlet, ribbon and badge of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") * Ribbon and badge of the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)") * Ribbon and insignia of the [Order of Merit of the Police Forces](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_the_Police_Forces "Order of Merit of the Police Forces") |
